input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation leverages a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach that covers requirements, functional, logical and product layers. It defines system structure and behavior with SysML, specifically SysML v1.7 IBDs for logical and product‑level architecture and state‑machine diagrams for dynamic behavior. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc capture the component‑and‑connector perspective; MontiArc Automaton is employed to model behavior automata. The modelling workflow integrates with simulation engines like MathWorks Simulink for dynamic and hybrid simulation. Source code and runtime components are generated in general‑purpose languages such as C++ and Java. Additionally, structured methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT are used to manage complexity across the development lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The twin system integrates horizontally with enterprise systems by leveraging standard interoperability frameworks: the Asset and Model services expose Asset Administration Shells (AAS) to provide a unified product data interface for MES and ERP systems. OPC UA is used for bi‑directional communication with legacy PLCs and field devices, while MQTT is employed for high‑frequency telemetry and event distribution to external controllers and dashboards. The system follows a publisher/subscriber pattern where edge gateways publish device data to MQTT topics; subscribers such as the Model service or external analytics dashboards consume these topics. The twin also offers HTTP/REST endpoints for on‑demand data retrieval, while CoAP is utilised for constrained devices. Integration with other Digital Twins is achieved through shared AAS references, enabling joint state queries and coordinated actions across the manufacturing ecosystem. Resource utilisation and connection health are monitored by an external Grafana dashboard that visualises MQTT broker metrics, OPC UA session counts, and AAS response times, ensuring seamless horizontal scaling and interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital twin computational models are defined across multiple abstraction layers using MBSE practices. Requirements and functional architecture are captured in SysML. Logical and product layers are detailed with architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML IBDs, providing component‑and‑connector viewpoints. Behavior is expressed through SysML v1.7 state machines or MontiArc Automaton, and higher‑fidelity dynamic behavior is realized by translating these models into Simulink block diagrams or equivalent simulation tools. Exported models are turned into executable code (C++ or Java). Relationships between models are maintained by component composition, interface specifications, and data‑flow mapping, ensuring direct traceability between physical sensor information, analytical equations, and geometrically‑based CAD representations within the twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The first robotic axis is driven by a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) coupled to a planetary‑gear transmission and a cycloidal gear stage. The motor is commanded by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D SL programmable motion controller, which implements vector‑control, torque‑limited driving and safety interlocks. The actuator chain exhibits a maximum angular velocity of 75°/s, defined by the motor speed limits and gear reduction, and the mechanical range of motion is bounded by the gearbox limits. Remote command of position, velocity and acceleration set‑points is delivered to the controller over an Ethernet/IP fieldbus, while the controller streams motor‑side measurements (position, torque, temperature) back to the Digital Twin via a real‑time data abstraction layer. Safety constraints include over‑current protection, collision detection, and emergency stop capability, all enforced by the controller firmware and mirrored in the twin’s safety model.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Each axis is instrumented with two incremental magnetic rotary encoders: a motor‑attached primary encoder mounted directly on the PMSM shaft, and a secondary encoder placed after the gearbox to provide absolute position. Encoder signals are sampled synchronously by the SINUMERIK controller at a high update rate (typically 10 kHz), producing a real‑time time‑series of joint positions. The controller streams all sensor data (encoders, current, temperature, vibration) to the Digital Twin over a 100 Mbps Ethernet/IP link; timestamps are attached with micro‑second resolution to enable precise alignment. The Twin aggregates these streams into a unified data set, facilitating closed‑loop monitoring, state estimation and validation of the computational model.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through a multi‑stage validation pipeline that compares simulated joint trajectories, motor electrical signals, and static geometry against the corresponding real‑time sensor measurements obtained from the encoder and other sensors. Residuals are quantified (e.g., mean absolute error, percentage deviation) and any deviation that exceeds a predefined tolerance magnitude triggers an auto‑calibration routine that adjusts model parameters (gear backlash, motor constants). Sources of uncertainty—such as encoder back‑lash, gear compliance, temperature drift and manufacturing tolerances—are identified, quantified via sensitivity analysis, and explicitly incorporated into the analytical models. Continuous cross‑checking of simulation and measurement streams is performed; anomalous data (e.g., out‑of‑range samples or loss‑of‑lock) are logged and prompt a safety shutdown. The overall approach follows IEC 61508‑based safety integrity level (SIL 4) guidelines for safety‑critical actuators and incorporates a dedicated data‑quality protocol that monitors sample integrity, missing data, and sensor drift, ensuring the Digital Twin operates with verified accuracy and traceability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The digital twin receives a continuous, automated one‑way data flow from the physical machinery—specifically a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D SL CNC controller—via an MQTT broker (MQTT 3.1.1). Sensor measurements (e.g., spindle speed, torque, positional data) are published in JSON format with a typical publication frequency of 10 Hz. Data are pre‑processed on the subscriber side by applying type validation, duplicate elimination, and timestamp alignment before being ingested into the twin’s data model. The integration pipeline utilizes a lightweight ETL layer that maps raw MQTT payloads directly to corresponding twin entities, ensuring temporal consistency and data quality through checksum verification.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between the physical device and the digital twin is established through a TLS‑secured MQTT 3.1.1 broker on the plant’s local area network. Messages are sent with Quality‑of‑Service level 1 to guarantee at least once delivery. The network topology is a flat star, with the broker providing a hub for multiple sensor topics. Bandwidth utilization remains below 200 kB/s per sensor stream, and typical end‑to‑end latency is under 50 ms, satisfying real‑time monitoring constraints.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle is structured into four abstraction phases. Phase 1 – Requirements: capture functional and non‑functional needs with SysML requirement diagrams. Phase 2 – Functional architecture: model component interfaces and high‑level interactions using SysML block definition diagrams or ADLs such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc. Phase 3 – Logical architecture: refine component behaviour with SysML v1.7 state machines or MontiArc automata, establishing connectors and messaging contracts. Phase 4 – Product/technical layer: transform the logical model into executable artefacts – Simulink models for simulation or C++/Java code for deployment. Transitions are managed through refinement and traceability links; lifecycle governance is achieved through MBSE practices, model validation, and stakeholder reviews.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering and evolution of a Digital Twin rely on Model‑Based Systems Engineering practices. Established methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT guide the iterative design of functional, logical, and physical aspects. Components are described with architectural description languages – ACME, AADL, MontiArc – capturing connector and interface semantics. Behaviour is encoded in SysML state machines (v1.7), MontiArc automata, or MathWorks Simulink models, with possible code generation into C++ or Java. Quality assurance occurs through simulation‑based validation, model consistency checks, and code review. Although explicit version control mechanisms are not listed, the formal, traceable model artefacts support incremental refactoring, regression testing, and evolution management across successive model releases.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

Therefore, the extraction is incorrect.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text describes specific services provided by the system. The Abstract mentions 'visualization in a dashboard' and 'live-data linking'. Section IV.A identifies the digital shadow's purpose as 'understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss' to improve control accuracy. While the paper distinguishes the Digital Shadow from a fully active Digital Twin, these functionalities (visualization, monitoring, analysis) constitute services provided to the user, making the extraction incorrect.

The extraction is highly specific and accurately reflects the tools and methodologies listed in Section II.A of the source text. It correctly identifies MBSE, SysML, various ADLs, and simulation engines. Quote: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML... RFLP... OOSEM... SPES... or SMArDT' and 'ACME... AADL... MontiArc... MathWorks Simulink'.

modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,

and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
